text: The surgeon positions the greater omentum cranially under the liver to expose the root of the small bowel mesentery, transverse mesocolon, and duodenum. Ileocolic vessels are identified, and a tonsil swab is placed inferiorly to prevent small bowel dislocation. The robotic arms are then docked in the specified arrangement.

The robotic arm R-I holds a fenestrated bipolar grasper, while the camera is positioned in R-II. Monopolar scissors are in R-III, and R-IV holds a grasper for macro-retraction. The surgeon begins by identifying the duodenum, using R-IV to retract the cecum for visualization of the ileocolic pedicle. A horizontal incision is made in the peritoneum below the ileocolic artery fold, revealing the loose areolar plane in front of the duodenum. Dissection proceeds above and lateral to the C of the duodenum, lifting the mesocolon away from retroperitoneal structures and continuing cranially until the colon is visible.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic pedicle, right colic vessels, and potentially the right branch of the middle colic artery, securing them with locking clips both proximally and distally. Monopolar energy is used for dissection, following the embryological plane, while ensuring the right plane is defined next to the pancreatic head to prevent injury. The mesocolon is mobilized medially to laterally, using the vessels as a tent to visualize retroperitoneal structures such as the duodenum and pancreatic head. Anterior vascular dissection is performed after adequate mobilization to secure retroperitoneal structures.

A tonsil swab is placed at the cranial limit of dissection near the hepatic flexure to protect the pancreas and duodenum from diathermy injury. The surgeon performs subidal dissection, dividing congenital adhesions caudal to the terminal ileum. The dissection continues cranially between the ascending colon and Gerota's fascia, simultaneously dividing lateral attachments.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the gyrotus fascia and ascending colon to prevent damage to retroperitoneal structures such as gonadal vessels and ureters. The right paracolic gutter dissection is facilitated by prior medial to lateral mesocolon mobilization. Monopolar energy is used to follow embryological planes, while bipolar energy is employed to control minor lateral vessels.

The surgeon completes the current stage as the liver edge becomes visible cranially and the duodenum medially. The next step involves entering the lesser sac from above by dividing the attachments between the greater omentum and the proximal transverse colon, facilitating superior dissection.

The swab placed from below becomes visible, guiding further lateral dissection to clear the hepatic flexure. The right branch of the middle colic artery may become visible and can be divided if necessary. The fully mobilized right colon specimen is extracted through a four-centimeter incision at the previous umbilical site, using a wound retractor to protect the edges from cancer cell spillage. An extracorporeal anastomosis is performed using the preferred technique.
